summary: 1. The chapter argues that successful projects are built around **“some” rather than “all”**: some features, some time, some knowledge, and some value for some people. 2. It presents the **Agile manifesto** as an intentionally asymmetrical…

title: Chapter11 Boss
summary: 1. The chapter argues that if a project is really happening, it will generate **problems, surprises, and bad news**, because reality never unfolds exactly as planned. 2. For bosses, this bad news should be seen not just as embarrassment or…

title: Chapter12 Conclusion
summary: 1. The conclusion argues that many common beliefs about projects are **things we “know that just ain’t so,”** especially the idea that success simply means being on time and on budget. 2. The book’s central aim is to replace these…

title: Chapter1 Stream

title: Chapter2 Swamp
summary: 1. The chapter compares projects to **building in a swamp**: they happen in complex, uncertain environments full of hidden risks and competing interests. 2. A project is affected not just by direct users or sponsors, but by a wide range of…

title: Chapter3 Escape
summary: 1. The chapter argues that projects become real when teams get **working software** into the hands of actual users as early as possible. 2. Early working software changes the project from **push** mode, where sponsors drive decisions, to…

title: Chapter4 Ship
summary: 1. The chapter argues that projects only improve when teams **notice problems, say them aloud, and try to fix or escalate them**. 2. Teams often avoid difficult issues by falling into **“agreed activity”**: work that feels safe and busy…

title: Chapter5 Car
summary: 1. The chapter uses **driving a car** as a metaphor for project management, especially for understanding how teams learn from experience. 2. It explains **empirical process** as working from real-world evidence: noticing what is happening,…

title: Chapter6 Bet
summary: 1. The chapter argues that **every project is a bet**: a decision made under uncertainty with the possibility of reward but also a real chance of failure. 2. Projects often begin because someone eventually stops analyzing, accepts the…

title: Chapter7 Notbet
summary: 1. The chapter argues that some projects are **not really bets at all** because they have been loaded with so many extra obstacles that they are almost certain to fail. 2. It calls these doomed projects **“nonsense accumulators”**: a…

title: Chapter8 Commit
summary: 1. The chapter argues that project management is often wrongly reduced to **keeping promises**, especially being on time and on budget, even though the real measure of success is whether the project delivers value. 2. It explains that…
